Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack see [http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).)
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Please find attached my ssh public key
Patric de Gentile-Williams
+44 7824 691484
----Original Message----
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 09 October 2024 14:40
To: P.M.W. De Gentile-Williams <pmwd2@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (pmwd2)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3) facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8, where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on that type) you may alternatively SSH to login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes, but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system but present additional development packages tuned to the node hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module (hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack see [http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).)

===============

MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Csh2001%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C45f1802bed5f4794f55108dc54b19708%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638478368922986884%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=CWOdOMH0UPXG%2FbLcyKonQ8u7LWG%2Bi%2BDUA6zg0BQ6u4M%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Csh2001%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C45f1802bed5f4794f55108dc54b19708%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638478368922986884%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=CWOdOMH0UPXG%2FbLcyKonQ8u7LWG%2Bi%2BDUA6zg0BQ6u4M%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3420MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3) facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids

Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S

For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7 Cascade Lake login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development work for the RHEL7 Cascade Lake cluster. login-icelake will take you to a different set of RHEL8 login nodes best suited for development work for Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes, but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519

SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).

=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home directory).

Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create global system issues.

================

E.g. if you delete a file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system but present additional development packages tuned to the node hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module (hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack see [http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).)
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

user_input:
Dear HPC Support team,
could I receive advice on using checkpoints when running a slurm script for AlphaFold 2.3.2?
I have found the following documentation useful:[https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html](https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html)
sbatch
Several options have been added to support checkpoint restart:
- --checkpoint: Specifies the interval between periodic checkpoint of a batch job. By default, the job will have no checkpoints created. Acceptable time formats include "minutes", "minutes:seconds", "hours:minutes:seconds", "days-hours", "days-hours:minutes" and "days-hours:minutes:seconds".
- --checkpoint-dir:Specify the directory when the checkpoint image files of a batch job will be stored. The default value is the current working directory. Checkpoint files will be of the form "<job_id>.ckpt" for jobs and "<job_id>.<step_id>.ckpt" for job steps.
Environment variables are available for all of these options:
SLURM_CHECKPOINT is equivalent to --checkpoint:
- SLURM_CHECKPOINT_DIR is equivalent to --checkpoint-dir
however, the --checkpoint command does not work from a script of from terminal. According to the slurm-rest-23.02 update log (which I believe is the current version of slurm on HPC):
"* The checkpoint plugin interface and all associated API calls have been removed."
Does this mean it is not possible to use checkpoints on HPC?
Thank you for your help,
Lisa
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Resilience / Checkpointing Policy
Technical Action Required: No (capability clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User asked about Slurm native `--checkpoint` functionality. Platform does not provide native Slurm checkpoint plugin support; users must implement checkpointing at application/workflow level. `dmtcp` module exists but compatibility with AlphaFold is user-dependent.

ACTION
1) Clarify no native Slurm checkpoint plugin on this cluster.
2) Recommend user-level checkpoint/restart implementation.
3) Mention `dmtcp` as optional tool to evaluate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Lisa,

Native Slurm checkpoint/restart is not enabled on this cluster, so `--checkpoint` options are not available here.

Checkpointing must be implemented at application/workflow level. We do provide a `dmtcp` module, though compatibility with AlphaFold would need to be tested in your workflow.

Kind regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply Slurm-native checkpoint flags are supported when plugin is absent.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SLURM / Checkpointing
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeking advice on using checkpoints with SLURM for AlphaFold 2.3.2. The retrieved documents indicate that checkpointing has been deprecated in recent SLURM versions, and DMTCP is used for long jobs [1][2][3][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm the current SLURM version and checkpointing capabilities.
2. Provide guidance on using DMTCP for checkpointing long jobs, as documented [4].
3. Verify if the user's script aligns with the DMTCP usage guidelines [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the current SLURM version on the HPC system?
- Can you provide the exact SLURM script you are using for AlphaFold 2.3.2?
- Have you tried using DMTCP for checkpointing as per the long jobs documentation [4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Lisa,

Thank you for your query. The checkpointing feature in SLURM has been deprecated in recent versions [1][2]. However, you can use DMTCP for checkpointing long jobs as documented here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/long.html#definition-of-long-jobs](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/long.html#definition-of-long-jobs) [4].

Please ensure your SLURM script aligns with the DMTCP usage guidelines. If you encounter any issues, please provide the exact SLURM script you are using for AlphaFold 2.3.2, and we will assist you further.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not use deprecated SLURM checkpointing features [1][2].
- Follow the documented guidelines for using DMTCP for long jobs [4].

[TICKET_SUMMARY] SLURM job checkpointing

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Does CSD3 Skylake-cpu currently have an available or preferred means to checkpoint and resume jobs? (i.e. save an image of the currently running job, end current slurm job and submit the image as a new slurm job, resuming as if there had been no interruption)
This would be a useful feature, as several members of my group have different applications that have (stochastic) run times exceeding the 12 or 36 hour cutoffs.
While we could modify every application individually to have it save and reload its state, it would be preferable if there was a general way to achieve this.
I can't see anything on this subject listed on the CSD3 documentation.
This might have the features required:
[https://slurm.schedmd.com/checkpoint_blcr.html](https://slurm.schedmd.com/checkpoint_blcr.html)
however it is listed as deprecated (although only as of a more recent version of slurm than CSD3 runs).

tint=5
echo "Start coordinator"
date
eval "dmtcp_coordinator --daemon --coord-logfile dmtcp_log.txt --exit-after-ckpt --exit-on-last -i "$tint" --port-file cport.txt -p 0"
sleep 2
cport=$(<cport.txt)
echo "$cport"
export DMTCP_COORD_HOST=$h

if [ -f "$RESTARTSCRIPT" ]

else

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU Software Build / NVHPC Toolchain
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The build is failing because the user is loading a very old NVHPC toolchain (`nvhpc/21.1`). The linker errors (`crtbegin.o`, `-latomic`, `-lstdc++`) indicate that this compiler stack is not compatible with the current Rocky8/Ampere software environment. The correct fix is to switch to the current Ampere base stack and a newer NVHPC module, then retry the install.

ACTION
1) Purge the current modules.
2) Load the current Ampere software stack:
   `module purge`
   `module load rhel8/ampere/base`
   `module load nvhpc/25.3/gcc/nv5pif7f`
3) Re-activate the virtual environment and rerun the `pip install` command.
4) If the build still fails, request the full updated build log and `module list` output.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Tom,

The failure is caused by the old `nvhpc/21.1` module stack rather than by the `jaxdecomp` package itself.

Please try:
`module purge`
`module load rhel8/ampere/base`
`module load nvhpc/25.3/gcc/nv5pif7f`

Then reactivate your environment and rerun the install.

If it still fails, please send the updated build log and the output of `module list`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Prefer the current Rocky8/Ampere base stack for Ampere GPU software builds.
- Do not recommend continuing with the deprecated NVHPC 21.1 stack.

user_input:
Dear HPC support team,
My name is Daniel Temko. I am a Research Associate in Prof. Jamie Blundell's group. I am currently trying to install a Singularity container to run AlphaFold3 on the HPC by using the attached Slurm script which I received from a recent CSD3-AI/ML clinic I attended. I am running into a problem (described below) and would be grateful for your help with trying to fix this.
Issue:
The script seems to have run successfully to install the container to /home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/Alphafold3. However, when I navigate to this directory and try to run the container by calling "./run_alphafold3" I get the following error message:
FATAL: permission denied
I was wondering whether you have a sense of why this might be happening and what I would need to do to get this working without the error.
Many thanks and kind regards,
Daniel
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Containers / AlphaFold / Singularity
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The AlphaFold3 container appears to have been created, but the user gets `FATAL: permission denied` when running `./run_alphafold3`. From the visible query alone, the safest interpretation is that the generated launcher script, its execute permissions, or the way it invokes the container needs checking. The next step is to inspect the wrapper script, confirm it is executable, and verify the image/data paths being used.

ACTION
1) Ask the user to show the generated launcher script and its permissions, e.g.:
   - `ls -l run_alphafold3`
   - `head -n 40 run_alphafold3`
2) Confirm the container image exists in the install directory and is readable.
3) If the launcher is not executable, instruct the user to run `chmod +x run_alphafold3`.
4) Review whether the launcher is invoking Singularity correctly for the local installation.
5) Confirm the required AlphaFold data/model weights are available and mapped as documented.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Daniel,

The `permission denied` error suggests the generated launcher script or the way it is being invoked needs checking.

Please send us:
- the output of `ls -l run_alphafold3`
- the first part of the script (`head -n 40 run_alphafold3`)
- confirmation that the AlphaFold image file exists in the install directory

If the launcher is not executable, please also try:
`chmod +x run_alphafold3`

Once we can see the wrapper contents and permissions, we can confirm the correct Singularity invocation.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume the container image is broken if the wrapper script was generated incorrectly.
- For AlphaFold3, confirm that model weights access has been approved separately.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Singularity Container
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The "FATAL: permission denied" error when trying to run the Singularity container for AlphaFold3 is a known issue that has been encountered by other users [2][3]. This issue is likely related to the installation path or permissions.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the installation path and permissions for the Singularity container.
2. Check if the user has enough space in their `hpc-work` directory.
3. Ensure the user is using the most recent implementation of the installation script [2].
4. If the issue persists, escalate to a higher-level support team member for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm the exact installation path used for the Singularity container?
- Have you checked the permissions for the directory where the container is installed?
- Have you verified that there is enough space in your `hpc-work` directory?
- Are you using the most recent installation script provided by Henry Carr?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Daniel,

Thank you for reaching out to the HPC support team. The "FATAL: permission denied" error you are encountering is a known issue that has been reported by other users [2][3]. This issue is likely related to the installation path or permissions.

To resolve this, please follow these steps:
1. Verify the installation path and permissions for the Singularity container.
2. Check if you have enough space in your `hpc-work` directory.
3. Ensure you are using the most recent implementation of the installation script provided by Henry Carr [2].

If the issue persists, please provide the following information:
- The exact installation path used for the Singularity container.
- The permissions for the directory where the container is installed.
- Confirmation that there is enough space in your `hpc-work` directory.
- Confirmation that you are using the most recent installation script.

We will escalate the issue to a higher-level support team member if necessary.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0004 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hello Daniel,
Can I confirm that you have done the following in your terminal?

```
# Generate run macro run_alphafold3
cat <<EOF> run_alphafold3
#!/bin/bash
SIMAGE=$INSTALL_DIR/alphafold3_3.0.1.sif 
DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/3

singularity exec --env \\
  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \\
  -B \$DATA:/data \\
  -B .:/etc
  --pwd /app/alphafold \\
  --nv \${SIMAGE} \\
  python run_alphafold.py \\
  --db_dir=/data
  "\$@"
EOF

chmod +x run_alphafold3
# Use as is: ./run_alphafold3
# with only alphafold3 option flags not described in the script body above.
```
This should create `run_alphafold3` which should work with the gpus.
Best regards,
Elisabeth

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hello Daniel,
Do you require support on this still? If not, I will close this ticket.
Best regards,
Elisabeth Reeve

Dear Sir/Madam,
I hope this email finds you well. I was trying to use BerkeleyGW on exciton calculations. It was very difficult because I have no experience with compilation BerkeleyGW on nvidia nodes before. Therefore, I am seeking your expertise on compilation BerkeleyGW on CSD3.
Thanks a lot!
Best wishes,
Bingcan
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Installation / Central Module Provisioning
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help running BerkeleyGW 4.0 on Ampere GPU nodes but does not have experience building it there. The best initial support path is to check whether a supported central build/module already exists and, if not, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team rather than relying on the user to complete a complex manual GPU build unaided.

ACTION
1) Confirm the exact version/variant required.
2) Check whether a supported central BerkeleyGW module already exists for Ampere.
3) If a supported module exists, send the user the relevant module-load instructions.
4) If no module exists, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bingcan,

We will check whether there is already a supported BerkeleyGW build/module available for Ampere.

If there is, we will send you the exact load commands. If not, we will need the precise version and build requirements so that we can advise on the next step or escalate appropriately.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Compilation [1][2][3][4][5]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][2][3][4][5]
Escalation Required: Yes (RSE team) [1]
Urgency: Medium [1][2][3][4][5]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeking assistance with compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3. Previous tickets indicate that similar compilation issues have been resolved with specific module loads and configuration files [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Escalate to the RSE team for detailed guidance on BerkeleyGW compilation [1].
2. Provide the user with initial steps based on previous successful resolutions [1].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Bingcan,

Thank you for your message. We will consult with our RSE team to provide detailed guidance on compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3. In the meantime, please try the following steps which have been successful for other users:

1. Run the following commands to load the necessary modules:
```
module purge
module load rhel8/cclake/base
module load intel-oneapi-compilers/2023.2.4/gcc/4lbvg4hv
module load hdf5/1.14.3/intel/intel-oneapi-mpi/si2tlq4v
module load intel-oneapi-mkl-cluster/2024.1.0/intel/r55ddo3z
```
2. Use the attached `arch.mk` file for configuration.
3. Ensure you run the above module commands from your job script when using the produced binaries.

We will get back to you with further details from the RSE team.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user is provided with accurate and tested module loads and configuration files [1].
- Escalate to the RSE team for complex compilation issues [1].

 module load PrgEnv-gnu

module load mkl
module list

1) craype-x86-rome            3) craype-network-ofi       5) xpmem/2.5.2-2.4_3.30__gd0f7936.shasta   7) epcc-setup-env     9) gcc/11.2.0     11) cray-dsmml/0.2.2   13) PrgEnv-gnu/8.3.3    15) cray-python/3.9.13.1

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Compiling Quantum Espresso on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi there,  
I have been using Quantum Espresso (v6.4 or v6.3) on CSD3 for a while. For the electron-phonon calculations, I realized that a part of the code is not parallized and I modified one of the subroutines and parallelized it with MPI on another machine. The calculations that took 50 hours before are now taking around 4 hours when use 20 cores. (It's not perfect but it is much better)  
I haven't tested my implementation on other machines or with multiple nodes. However, I want to try to implement it on CSD3 since I have less than 3 weeks for my MPhil thesis submission and I need to run some heavy calculations.  
I will be glad if you can help me compile it on my home directory or as a module. The subroutine I am changing is at "/PHonon/PH/elphon.f90" script inside QE directories. I attached my modified script.  
Looking forward to hear back from you.  
Best regards, 
Kem 
  
--- 
Kemal Atalar 
CDT Computational Methods in Materials Science Student 
Cavendish Laboratory 
University of Cambridge 
Cambridge 3B3 0HE

./install_cp2k_toolchain.sh --with-openmpi --with-openblas --with-scalapack --with-libxsmm --with-elpa --with-sirius=no --with-gcc
Best Regards,
IniOluwa C. Popoola,
PhD Student, ICE Group,
Yusuf Hamied Department of Chemistry,
Gates Cambridge Scholar,
Churchill College, University of Cambridge, UK.
Please note that although I may choose to send emails outside office hours, you should not feel any pressure to respond outside of your own working hours.

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Compiling Quantum Espresso on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi there,  
I have been using Quantum Espresso (v6.4 or v6.3) on CSD3 for a while. For the electron-phonon calculations, I realized that a part of the code is not parallized and I modified one of the subroutines and parallelized it with MPI on another machine. The calculations that took 50 hours before are now taking around 4 hours when use 20 cores. (It's not perfect but it is much better)  
I haven't tested my implementation on other machines or with multiple nodes. However, I want to try to implement it on CSD3 since I have less than 3 weeks for my MPhil thesis submission and I need to run some heavy calculations.  
I will be glad if you can help me compile it on my home directory or as a module. The subroutine I am changing is at "/PHonon/PH/elphon.f90" script inside QE directories. I attached my modified script.  
Looking forward to hear back from you.  
Best regards, 
Kem 
  
--- 
Kemal Atalar 
CDT Computational Methods in Materials Science Student 
Cavendish Laboratory 
University of Cambridge 
Cambridge 3B3 0HE

Benjamin

Dear HPC support,
I would like to use the interactive Rstudio (via [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk<https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%3Chttps://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)>), but I need a newer R version. The version that it loads is 4.3.1, whereas I would like 4.5.1. Is it possible to get the interactive Rstudio to load this R version?
Thank you!
Best,
Heather
–
Heather Machado
Group Leader
CRUK Career Development Fellow
Department of Pathology
Department of Genetics
University of Cambridge
Cambridge, UK
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login-Web / RStudio Service Configuration
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants Login-Web RStudio to use R 4.5.1 instead of the currently configured 4.3.1. The safest answer is to say that this is not currently possible with the Login-Web RStudio service as configured and to recommend alternative workflows where appropriate.

ACTION
1) Confirm that Login-Web RStudio cannot presently be switched to R 4.5.1.
2) Do not promise a timeline for service reconfiguration.
3) Offer an alternative such as Jupyter or a non-Login-Web workflow if the user needs a newer R version.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Heather,

It is not currently possible to make the Login-Web RStudio service use R 4.5.1. The service is constrained by the way it is configured.

If you need that newer R version immediately, please use an alternative workflow such as a different interactive environment rather than Login-Web RStudio.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / RStudio
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user requests to use a newer version of R (4.5.1) in the interactive RStudio session. There is evidence of users requesting and using different versions of R, but no specific mention of version 4.5.1. The process involves loading the desired R version via module commands.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if R version 4.5.1 is available as a module.
2. If available, provide the user with the module load command for R 4.5.1.
3. If not available, escalate to the technical team to check the feasibility of installing R 4.5.1.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm if you have tried loading any other versions of R using the module load command?
- Are there any specific packages or dependencies that require R 4.5.1?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Heather,

Thank you for your query. To use a newer version of R in the interactive RStudio session, you can load the desired version using the module load command.

Please try the following steps:
1. Log in to the HPC system.
2. Load the desired R version using the command: `module load R/4.5.1`
3. Load RStudio using the command: `module load RStudio`
4. Launch RStudio with the command: `rstudio`

If R 4.5.1 is not available, we will need to check with our technical team about the possibility of installing it.

Please let us know if you encounter any issues or if you have further questions.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user is aware of the module load commands and how to use them.
- If the requested R version is not available, escalate to the technical team for further action.

REFERENCE KEY

user_input:
Hi,
I am trying to run multi-xpu fine-tuning on the DAWN intel XPUs. I almost succeeded but there is an error raised.
According to Google, I have to install `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` to fix it.
[https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9](https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9)
The problem is that I do not have the sudo authentication.
Could you please help me install this package?
Thank you 
Best wishes,
Jiangnan
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN / Intel XPU / oneAPI Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Package Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is attempting to run multi-XPU fine-tuning on DAWN Intel XPUs and requires the installation of `level-zero-dev` to resolve an error. The user does not have sudo authentication to install the package themselves.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if `level-zero-dev` can be installed via environment modules or if it needs to be added to the DAWN image.
2. If installation via environment modules is possible, provide the user with the necessary module load commands.
3. If installation via environment modules is not possible, escalate the request to the system administrators for inclusion in the DAWN image.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact error message you are encountering?
- Have you tried any other methods to install the required package?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Jiangnan,

Thank you for your request. We will need to verify if the `level-zero-dev` package can be installed via environment modules or if it needs to be added to the DAWN image.

In the meantime, could you please provide the exact error message you are encountering? Additionally, have you tried any other methods to install the required package?

We will get back to you as soon as we have more information.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any package installations comply with the system's security and compatibility policies.
- Avoid providing sudo access to users for package installations.

Good morning,
I’ve managed to get a large proportion of our code codes ported to GPU using JAX, an appropriate framework for our needs, which shows excellent performance at large scale.
It would be fantastic if these calculations could be run on DAWN. However, as documented in this thread, I have not had any success in installing it.
I understand there is no reason why it should not work on Intel machines, see the stable diffusion example below which is run on a similar hardware configuration as DAWN.
[https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html)

Many thanks,
Lewis

In short, my questing is; is it even possible for someone who knows what they are doing to get to the “Check that PJRT Intel GPU Plugin is loaded” from here: [https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html)

Good morning,
I’ve managed to get a large proportion of our code codes ported to GPU using JAX, an appropriate framework for our needs, which shows excellent performance at large scale.
It would be fantastic if these calculations could be run on DAWN. However, as documented in this thread, I have not had any success in installing it.
I understand there is no reason why it should not work on Intel machines, see the stable diffusion example below which is run on a similar hardware configuration as DAWN.
[https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html)
It would be great if I could have some support in doing this, as I think our group and other potential JAX/DAWN users will benefit greatly from this.
Many thanks,
Lewis

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Source Build / User-Space Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help installing Toast++ in their own workspace after an earlier unsuccessful attempt. This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a request for a central software install. The best initial helpdesk response is to gather any previous build errors and, if needed, attempt a clean user-space build internally before sending tested instructions.

ACTION
1) Confirm the source/version the user is trying to build.
2) Ask for any error output from the user's earlier build attempt if available.
3) If needed, attempt a clean user-space build in `hpc-work` and return tested instructions.
4) Prefer a user-space install unless there is evidence of wider demand for a central package.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Liam,

This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a central software installation.

If you still have the output from your previous build attempt, please send it to us. If needed, we can also try a clean build internally and then send you tested instructions for building Toast++ in your workspace.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Hello,
Is there any chance we can add a module for the last version of blast? I think the last one available is 2.11, and it is not complete (for example blastp is missing)
Thanks.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Request / Module Availability
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a newer BLAST module because the currently visible version appears incomplete. The correct initial helpdesk response is to treat this as a software request: check whether a newer supported module already exists and, if not, investigate whether one can be added.

ACTION
1) Check whether a newer supported BLAST module already exists.
2) If not, investigate the request as a software-installation/update request.
3) If a newer module becomes available, send the exact module name and load command to the user.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello,

We can investigate whether a newer BLAST module can be provided.

If we are able to add a newer supported version, we will send you the exact module name and load command.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Module Request
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting an update to the BLAST module to include the latest version and ensure all components (such as blastp) are available. The current version (2.11) is incomplete, and there is a precedent for updating modules to newer versions [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Escalate the request to the HPC support team for module update.
2. Verify the latest version of BLAST and ensure it includes all necessary components.
3. Build and test the new module.
4. Update the module repository with the new version.
5. Notify the user once the update is complete.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for your request. We will escalate this to our HPC support team to update the BLAST module to the latest version, ensuring all components are included. We will notify you once the update is complete.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the new module is thoroughly tested before deployment.
- Communicate any delays or issues to the user promptly.

REFERENCE KEY

[2] : HPCSSUP-32047::chunk::0002

[CONTEXT]

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0002b role=TICKET_CREATOR part=2/2 original_id=0002>
---
 
Von: Leon Bond <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Gesendet: Mittwoch, 3. Februar 2021 17:43
An: M. Nackenhorst <mn480@cam.ac.uk>
Betreff: HPCSSUP-32047 remote blast against nt database fails 
   
—-—-—-—
Reply above this line.
Leon Bond commented:
Dear Maja,
I believe that the version of blast we have on the cluster is too old (v2.4) to make secure connections to the remote site, i.e. https:// instead of http://
The blast documentation sugggests that v2.5+ is needed for this ([1][https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/))
We've built a v2.9 module of blast, which you can try by running
module load /usr/local/software/spack/current/share/spack/tcl/linux-scientific7-skylake_avx512/blast-plus-2.9.0-gcc-9.3.0-h4vn5dr 
That isn't currently visible to users by default, as we've had to use a different version of the 'spack' software packaging tool for this. In the near future we'll be making changes that will allow this to be visible via a search.
Could you try using this module and see if it works for you instead of blast/2.4.0 ?
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services

Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment / Conda Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue described is a common problem with activating Conda environments in SLURM batch jobs. The provided SLURM script appears to follow the correct steps for loading the module and activating the environment, but the environment is not being recognized correctly within the job. This issue has been reported in similar cases [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the SLURM script for correct module loading and environment activation.
2. Ensure that the `conda init` command is used to initialize Conda in the script.
3. Check if the `.bashrc` file is being sourced correctly within the script.
4. If the issue persists, escalate to a higher-level support team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact error message from the SLURM job output?
- Have you tried running the script with `conda init` and sourcing `.bashrc` as shown in the provided examples?
- Are there any specific Conda packages that are not being recognized in the job?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Stefanos,

Thank you for your detailed query. The issue you're experiencing with Conda environment activation in SLURM batch jobs is a known problem. Here are some steps to troubleshoot and resolve the issue:

1. Ensure that your SLURM script includes the `conda init` command to initialize Conda.
2. Make sure that the `.bashrc` file is being sourced correctly within the script.
3. Verify that the module loading and environment activation commands are in the correct order.

Here is an example of a modified SLURM script based on your description:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake

# Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
module load miniconda

# Initialize Conda
conda init --all

# Source the .bashrc file
source ~/.bashrc

# Activate the environment
conda activate ml

# Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"

# Alternatively:
echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"

# Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
```

Please try running the modified script and let us know if the issue persists. If you encounter any specific error messages, please provide them for further assistance.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the `conda init` command is used to initialize Conda in SLURM scripts.
- Verify that the `.bashrc` file is being sourced correctly within the script.
- Do not guess the solution without verified evidence.

REFERENCE KEY

[3] : HPCSSUP-65220::chunk::0000
[4] : HPCSSUP-26914::chunk::0000
[5] : HPCSSUP-83117::chunk::0000

Dear Akshay,

module load rhel8/default-icl              # REQUIRED - loads the basic environment#! Insert additional module load commands after this line if needed: 
module load python/3.8 
module load miniconda/3

conda init --all 
source /home/aj686/.bashrc
conda activate euclid
```
Please change your submission script and try again. Let me know if this worked for you.
Kind regards,
Avraam

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Activating conda environment 

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear Support Team,
Hope this email finds you well.
I am writing to ask for help when activating a conda environment inside a SLURM job. I have submitted multiple jobs with the following script (activating the conda environment using `source activate`):
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J castep-xyz
#SBATCH -A MOYA-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks=28
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH -p icelake
#! Output filename:
#! %A means slurm job ID and %a means array index
#SBATCH --output=castep_alpha_1_%A.out
#! Errors filename:
#SBATCH --error=castep_alpha_1_%A.err
#SBATCH --mail-type=ALL
#! ###############################
#! Script to run CASTEP from ASE
#! ###############################
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge # Removes all modules still loaded
module load rhel8/default-icl # Loads the basic environment
module load castep/impi/22.11 # Load CASTEP
module load miniconda/3 # Load miniconda, required otherwise cannot activate conda env
source activate /home/jiq21/.conda/envs/conv
export I_MPI_PIN_DOMAIN=omp:compact
export I_MPI_PIN_ORDER=scatter
#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
numtasks=$SLURM_NTASKS
mpi_tasks_per_node=$(echo "$SLURM_TASKS_PER_NODE" | sed -e 's/^([0-9][0-9]).$/\1/')
#! Work directory (i.e. where the job will run):
workdir="$SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR" # The value of SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR sets workdir to the directory
1. in which sbatch is run.
#! Are you using OpenMP (NB this is unrelated to OpenMPI)? If so increase this
#! safe value to no more than 32:
#export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
#! Number of MPI tasks to be started by the application per node and in total (do not change):
np=$[${numnodes}*${mpi_tasks_per_node}]
cd $workdir
echo -e "Changed directory to `pwd`.\n"
JOBID=$SLURM_JOB_ID
ARRAYID=$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID
echo -e "JobID: $JOBID, ArrayID: $ARRAYID \n======"
echo "Time: `date`"
echo "Running on master node: `hostname`"
echo "Current directory: `pwd`"
echo "\nnumtasks=$numtasks, numnodes=$numnodes, mpi_tasks_per_node=$mpi_tasks_per_node (OMP_NUM_THREADS=$OMP_NUM_THREADS)"
command="mpirun -ppn $mpi_tasks_per_node -np $np castep.mpi"
pp_path="../../CASTEP_pspots/"
output_path="../../database-dft/"
traj_files=(*.lammpstrj) # get all lammpstrj files in the database
traj_file=${traj_files[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID-1]}
output_file=${output_path}/${traj_file%.lammpstrj}_dft_1.xyz # remove .xyz from the file name
1. alpha: 10 frames of equilibration, skip every other 2 frames (33)
2. --index="10:90:2" (40)
3. using pp_path, dir_path and task as default
python /home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/main_run_dft.py \
--command="${command}" \
--dirpath="../../CASTEP_calcs/${traj_file%.lammpstrj}_1/calc/" \
--pppath="${pp_path}" \
--filename="${traj_file}" \
--output="${output_file}" \
--task="singlepoint" \
--xcfunc="PBEsol" \
--cutoffenergy=750 \
--kp=0.028 \
--electol=1e-8 \
--index="11:77:2"
They were running without errors until yesterday, when I had the error:
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "/home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/main_run_dft.py", line 6, in <module>
from dft_calc import *
File "/home/jiq21/bin/myscripts/data_gen/src/dft_calc.py", line 5, in <module>
from ase import units, Atoms
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ase'
For the job with ID 5113464, which was an array job queued for 10 days. I have resent the job using: `sbatch —array=1 —time=00:01:00 -N1 -n1 sub_ase_castep_alpha_1` (script in the path: /home/jiq21/rds/hpc-work/year_3/AgI_mace_train_try2/md-database/1kbar/ , charged to account MOYA-SL3-CPU) and it ran without an environment problem (although obviously timed out before computing anything).
My worry is that I have multiple jobs with a similar script in the queue since the 12th and I am not sure whether they will run successfully or have a problem activating the conda environment. Would you be able to give any suggestions on why the previous job failed? I did not send it while the environment was activated in the login node. I had tried that previously and didn’t succeed on activating the environment.
Kind regards,
Javiera Quiroz
PhD student
Department of Material Science
University of Cambridge

user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Platform Incident / Filesystem Performance
Technical Action Required: Yes (investigate possible service-side performance issue)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Slow Conda environment setup and activation on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC Support Team,
I am a PhD student in the Engineering Department, division F, have been experiencing some significant delays with conda environment setup and activation on CSD3, and am attaching details below which I hope can be helpful in diagnosing the issue.
1. Environment build failures
I had to make three attempts to build a conda environment with PyTorch 2.8.0 and pyannote.audio 4.0.3. The first two jobs timed out despite using very little CPU or memory. The bottleneck appears to be network I/O when downloading packages. Here are the details:
Job ID
State
Wall-clock
CPU Used
Memory Used
21592679
TIMEOUT (killed during "Verifying transaction")
02:00:11
00:01:22 (0.28%)
357 MB (2.18%)
21596158
TIMEOUT (killed during "Installing pip dependencies")
04:00:08
00:02:00 (0.21%)
508 MB (3.10%)
21629759
COMPLETED
00:07:36
00:01:17 (3.38%)
278 MB (1.70%)
The third job completed quickly because the packages were already cached from the previous two attempts.
2. Conda activation and script runtime speed
Since last Thursday, activating conda environment has been notably slow. I had to remove the conda initialisation lines from my ~/.bashrc file in order to access the login node. I also tried requesting an interactive session and a CPU cluster node, but it could take 10–20 minutes to activate a conda environment. Things have been better today (around 3–5 minutes), but I thought it was still worth reporting in case the issue recurs.
Additionally, I have a testing script for the conda environment that finishes running in under 20 seconds (including initialisation time) on local machine, but takes 8–9 minutes to run on the login node or an interactive CPU session.
I am CC-ing colleagues in my team and @Jinghong Chen<[jc2124@cam.ac.uk](mailto:jc2124@cam.ac.uk)> and @Guangyu Yang<[gy266@cam.ac.uk](mailto:gy266@cam.ac.uk)>, whose team is experiencing similar conda-related slowness on the GPU cluster as well: a programme that is expected to finish in half an hour is taking over 6 hours to run.
I was wondering whether the slowness related to network I/O and conda is expected, and whether there is anything we are missing?
Many thanks and I look forward to hearing from you!
Best regards,
Shilin

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Cannot set-up conda environment on CSD3 HPC

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear CSD3 team,
I have encountered a hurdle when trying to set-up and run computations on your cluster for the first time.
I am unable to successfully set-up my Conda environment with the packages that I require to run my Python script. I have been working to resolve this issue for multiple days now but at no avail.
Initially, I started by trying to re-create the Conda environment that I currently work on within a different  compute cluster. When this did not work, I simply tried installing one package at a time, but would still encounter the same issue regardless of how simple I tried to make the process.
The error I keep encountering (many times, sometimes in slightly differing error messages) is that the environment is inconsistent and it fails to resolve. Here are the packages that I need to install to allow my code to run:
bctpy – Brain Connectivity Toolbox (Python version; installable via pip)
*
h5py
*
matplotlib
*
networkx
*
numpy
*
pandas
*
plotly
*
scikit-learn
*
scipy
*
seaborn
*
torch
*
umap-learn
I have tried different solutions including updating conda (was not able to do this, same issue) and installing mamba (was not able to do this, same issue). I am not very knowledgeable about these things - but I have been able to work with conda successfully on another compute cluster, no issue.
I would very much appreciate any help that you could give me. I am available at any time to provide any more information, or to work through this through videocall/screenshare if this is something that you do.
Thanks and best wishes,
El

Yesterday everything worked fine.
This affects all multiple machines I've provisioned (in interactive sessions).
Thank you in advance for your support!
- Konstantin Sturtzkopf

user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment Performance / Shared Storage
Technical Action Required: No (performance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

2. Escalate to the system administrators for further investigation if the issue is widespread.
3. Provide the user with a workaround if available, such as keeping the environment active or using a different partition for the virtual environment.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact timestamps when the issue occurs?
- Have you noticed any patterns or specific times when the issue is more frequent?
- Have you tried running the script on different nodes (e.g., CPU nodes instead of GPU nodes)?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

Thank you for reporting this issue. We have received similar reports from other users regarding intermittent delays when importing Python modules [1][2][3][4][5]. This issue may be related to file system latency or overall system load.

We are currently investigating the issue and will provide an update as soon as possible. In the meantime, you can try keeping your environment active or using a different partition for your virtual environment to see if it helps.

Please let us know if you have any additional information or if the issue persists.

Best regards,
HPC Support
