Dear support team,
I am trying to log in to my RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) using FileZilla. I am following the instructions given in "Reseach Storage Documentation”. Unfortunately, I am denied access after entering my password and the TOTP verification code (see screenshot).
I can succesfully log in to [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/), so I assume I have the correct password. I have also succesfully set up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 (see screenshot), and I am correctly entering the code when prompted to do so.
Maybe I need a different password that is specific for this service?
Kind regards
Christina
Prof. Dr. med. Christina Zielinski
Professor of Infection and Immunity
Department of Pathology
University of Cambridge
Tennis Court Road
Cambridge CB2 1QP
cez22@cam.ac.uk
s[Screenshot 2026-03-08 at 09.42.33.png]
[IMG_5398.PNG]
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RCS Access / Group Membership / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The access failure is unlikely to be explained by a separate service-specific password, especially since the user can log in elsewhere successfully. The most likely next steps are to check whether there is an access-control/group-membership problem and to confirm that the user has generated the correct MFA token for RCS/CSD3 access via `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

ACTION
1) Check whether the account has the correct storage/access-group membership.
2) Instruct the user to create the correct TOTP via:
   `ssh <crsid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
3) Share the MFA documentation.
4) Remind the user of the normal login path and storage path once access has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Christina,

You should not need a separate password for this service. We will check your storage access permissions on our side.

You will also need to create the MFA token that works with RCS/CSD3 access. Please run:
`ssh cez22@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the on-screen instructions. Guidance is here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Issues
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing access issues with RCS storage via FileZilla, despite successfully logging in to other HPC services and setting up MFA/TOTP. Similar issues have been reported in previous tickets [1][2][3][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's access permissions to the RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) [3][4].
2. Confirm that the user has correctly set up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 and that the codes are being entered correctly [1][2][3].
3. Check if there are any known issues with FileZilla or the RCS storage service that could be causing the access denial.
4. If the issue persists, escalate to the RCS team for further investigation [3][4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you have been added as a user to the RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) by the PI/owner of the project? [3][4]
- Have you tried accessing the RCS storage using a different SFTP client, such as Cyberduck, to see if the issue is specific to FileZilla? [2][4]
- Can you provide the exact error message you are receiving when attempting to log in via FileZilla? [1][2]

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Christina,

Thank you for your message. We will investigate the access issue with your RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) via FileZilla. In the meantime, please confirm that you have been added as a user to the RCS storage by the PI/owner of the project. Additionally, try accessing the storage using a different SFTP client, such as Cyberduck, to see if the issue is specific to FileZilla.

Please provide the exact error message you are receiving when attempting to log in via FileZilla. This will help us diagnose the issue more accurately.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user has the necessary permissions to access the RCS storage before proceeding with troubleshooting steps [3][4].
- Do not disclose sensitive information or credentials in the ticket or email correspondence.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-72734::chunk::0000
[2] : HPCSSUP-51042::chunk::0000
[3] : HPCSSUP-57950::chunk::0002
[4] : HPCSSUP-60259::chunk::0003

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi,
I am following your guide ([https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rcs/gui.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rcs/gui.html)) on how to connect to RCS. I have setup the site in FileZilla and am using the interactive password option. I am asked for my password which I enter, and then a TOTP Access Token. I presumed this was the one from the Microsoft Authenticator App, but when I enter the 6 digit code it says Access Denied and asks for my password again.
My password is correct because my computer is bound to blue ad as this is the same raven password.

I am then prompted to enter the TOTP MFA code/token (which I obtain from Google Authenticator, CSD3:SSH Login) but the authentication fails.
I tried connecting from work (cam wifi), from home (with and without vpn) and using different accounts to scan the QR in google authenticator (no account, my personal google account and my university email),

Thank you,
Eva

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0009 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Eva,
To be able to access shared storage space PI/owner of the project will need to add your crsid using self service storage portal. This will then generate ticket for us to grant you access and send out welcome email. Once all this is done you will be able to log in to rcs.uis.cam.ac.uk as expected.

Kind regards,
Greg
RCS Team

user_input:
Good evening,
I have recently been trying to connect to the CSD3 to start working in the cluster for the first time. I have Two-Factor Authentication on my account already enabled (please see image attached).
When I try to connect to the login node (login.hpc.cam.ac.uk) through ssh I am being requested to provide the TOTP Verification Code. Unfortunately, I did not retain the QR code when connecting for the first time to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Would it be possible to reset this multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code so I can scan it with Google Authenticator to set the login for my account?
Many thanks for your assistance on this matter.
Martin
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 27 January 2026 14:44
To: Martin Santamarina Garcia <ms3242@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (ms3242)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support

The user is unable to generate a new TOTP secret for their phone without first contacting support. This is a known issue and requires a reset of the multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Reset the multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code for the user.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

Thank you for your message. We have reset the multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code for your account. Please attempt the SSH connection again to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk to generate a new TOTP MFA secret.

If you encounter any further issues, please let us know.

Best regards,
HPC Support

From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Date:  Monday, 16 January 2023 at 13:59
To:  Barbara Walkowiak <bw450@cam.ac.uk>
Subject:  CSD3 systems at Cambridge (bw450)   
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
Peta4-CPU     - Intel Skylake/Omni-Path, Cascade Lake/IB
                and Ice Lake/IB CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR IB GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
  Skylake and Cascade Lake use a RHEL7-compatible O/S
  Ice Lake and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk 
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7,
OPA-enabled login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Peta4 clusters. login-icelake will take you to a
different set of RHEL8, Mellanox OFED and CUDA-enabled login nodes
best suited for development work for Ice Lake and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
    MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
    SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
    MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
    SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best
snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on skylake* and cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk. 
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see 
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.)](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.))
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU 
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
 
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
 
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
 
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
 
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
 
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and

Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk

information about MFA on CSD3 please see

All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA

SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI

SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjb2421%40cam.ac.uk%7C82f7b6d03d904420a89e08de8e309829%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639104536533408285%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=NH%2F8TvUmdepzNnPbD3oQqKraOI1OcZKwSH4ANDOOC9Q%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjb2421%40cam.ac.uk%7C82f7b6d03d904420a89e08de8e309829%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639104536533408285%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=NH%2F8TvUmdepzNnPbD3oQqKraOI1OcZKwSH4ANDOOC9Q%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake*, sapphire* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cadymaa2%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C1d28b6d227c64bcb690c08dc4e3fb374%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638471282699625864%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=IqRIdZ883a%2BzGzXOGpCBtdBCD5PW72NutW22ily2LvQ%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cadymaa2%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C1d28b6d227c64bcb690c08dc4e3fb374%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638471282699625864%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=IqRIdZ883a%2BzGzXOGpCBtdBCD5PW72NutW22ily2LvQ%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3420MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public

on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.

multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk

information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.

but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.

fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI

As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is

Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different

require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)

compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using

which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.

MPI was used) can be extracted with

it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Re: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (adymaa2)

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
What is the password for multifactor authentication? My raven password didn’t work and I didn’t receive a message in my Microsoft authenticator app with my crisid.
Best,
Abdulwahab Alshallal
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Date: Wednesday, March 27, 2024 at 9:24 AM
To: Abdulwahab Alshallal <adymaa2@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (adymaa2)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
Cascade Lake uses a RHEL7-compatible O/S
Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7
Cascade Lake login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Cascade Lake cluster. login-icelake will take you
to a different set of RHEL8 login nodes best suited for development
work for Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake*, sapphire* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cadymaa2%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C1d28b6d227c64bcb690c08dc4e3fb374%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638471282699625864%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=IqRIdZ883a%2BzGzXOGpCBtdBCD5PW72NutW22ily2LvQ%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cadymaa2%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C1d28b6d227c64bcb690c08dc4e3fb374%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638471282699625864%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=IqRIdZ883a%2BzGzXOGpCBtdBCD5PW72NutW22ily2LvQ%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3420MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

user_input: During my interactive session with RStudio, I noticed that if I am not active on the page while my model is running, I am logged out of the RStudio server, but the login I use for the HPC does not work to log back into the RStudio server. I have attempted to refresh the HPC main page, log out and back in, and then relaunch my RStudio session that is currently running. However, when I try to relaunch it, I am redirected to an RStudio login page that displays the message: “Temporary server error, please try again.”  When I attempt to log in using the same credentials I use for the HPC, it does not allow me to log back in. I have also tried Chrome and Safari, and seem to run into the same login page. My models still run, but I would like to be able to access the R page while they are running so I can see its progress. Thank you!
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / RStudio Session Reconnect
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to reconnect to an already running RStudio interactive session after the web page timed out. In this situation, the RStudio login prompt may require session-specific connection credentials rather than the user’s normal HPC password. The next step is to retrieve the connection details from the running session page and use the session password shown there.

ACTION
1) Direct the user to the page for the running session.
2) Retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there, including the password.
3) Use that session password at the RStudio login prompt instead of the normal HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Julia,

Please open the page for the running RStudio session and retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there.

Use the password provided on that session page at the RStudio login prompt rather than your normal HPC password.

If you still cannot reconnect, please send us a screenshot of the session page and the login prompt.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / RStudio
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing an issue with RStudio where they are logged out during an interactive session and cannot log back in using their HPC credentials. This issue has been reported by other users as well [1][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if there are any ongoing issues with the RStudio server.
2. Check the user's session status and logs for any errors.
3. If the issue persists, escalate to the RStudio support team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact error message you receive when trying to log back in?
- Have you tried logging in from a different browser or device?
- Can you share the session ID and any relevant logs?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for reaching out. We understand that you are experiencing issues with logging back into your RStudio session after being logged out. This issue has been reported by other users as well [1][4][5].

We will investigate the issue further and check if there are any ongoing problems with the RStudio server. In the meantime, please provide the exact error message you receive when trying to log back in and any relevant logs if possible.

If the issue persists, we may need to escalate this to the RStudio support team for further investigation.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that user credentials and session details are handled securely.
- Follow the escalation procedure if the issue cannot be resolved at the helpdesk level.

user_input:
Dear support team
I am having trouble accessing the RFS service for my lab group (TP group, Condensed Matter Physics, Maxwell Centre). It has already been mapped, and it was working fine, but today it asked me for the password of my username (of the computer I was using). As you know, those are generated tokens. So, I generated a new one for the computer I am using, but it does not accept it. How can we fix that? It is urgent that I have access to my data.
Best regards
Patricia N. Lazo Paravicini
Pilizota Lab
Department of Physics
University of Cambridge
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RFS Access / Credential Confusion
Technical Action Required: No (workflow clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to use a generated token/device password for RFS access on Windows. The correct fix is to ensure they are on the Cambridge VPN or network, follow the Windows RFS instructions, and use their normal UIS credentials for Access Blue rather than the VPN/device token.

ACTION
1) Confirm the user is on the Cambridge VPN or directly on the network.
2) Direct them to the Windows RFS guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html
3) Clarify that the username/password prompt is for normal UIS credentials, not the VPN/device token.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patricia,

Please make sure you are connected to the Cambridge VPN or the Cambridge network directly, and follow the Windows guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html

For this login prompt, you should use your normal UIS credentials rather than the generated VPN/device token.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RFS Access Issue
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: High

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues accessing the RFS service despite having a valid token. This issue is urgent as the user needs immediate access to their data. Similar issues have been reported where users were unable to access RFS due to incorrect username or password errors [1][2][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's RFS access credentials and ensure they are using the correct username and password.
2. Check if the user is using the correct RFS path and if the network drive connection is properly configured.
3. If the issue persists, escalate to the Research Computing Services team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm the exact RFS path you are trying to access?
- Have you tried using your University Account (Raven) password instead of the token password?
- Have you selected the 'More Choices' option and set the username to be your CRSid or BLUE\CRSid?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Patricia,

Thank you for reaching out. We understand that you are having trouble accessing the RFS service for your lab group. This issue is urgent, and we will do our best to resolve it promptly.

Please ensure that you are using your University Account (Raven) password instead of the token password. Additionally, when prompted for your username, select the 'More Choices' option and set the username to be your CRSid or BLUE\CRSid.

If the issue persists, please provide the exact RFS path you are trying to access, and we will escalate this to the Research Computing Services team for further investigation.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always verify the user's credentials and ensure they are using the correct username and password for RFS access.
- Escalate to the Research Computing Services team if the issue cannot be resolved through standard troubleshooting steps.

Hi,

but neither mine nor lh709's UIS passwords work (following instructions including BLUE).

Jamie

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Trouble accessing RFS

[CONTEXT]
@Leon Bond I’m not familiar with connecting to RFS, however, is there any possibility that there would be a password error here? Or is there something else amiss?

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0004 role=OTHER>
That’s their network token username they are trying to use (the `+g14_1` suffix gives it away), not their Raven one. Laptop probably saved the username from connecting to the VPN. They need to hit ‘more choices’ and enter their username as `ot284` or `BLUE\ot284` and the Raven password.

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0005 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hello,
Just following up on this!
Best,
Oscar
[FILE ATTACHMENT]

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0006 role=OTHER>
Hello Oscar,
In the screenshot, you are using the username generated for one of your network tokens (https://help.uis.cam.ac.uk/service/network-services/tokens ) - probably saved when you were setting up the VPN connection.
Could you select the ‘More Choices’ option at the bottom and set the username to be ot284 or BLUE\ot284 and use your University Account (Raven) password. That should allow you to connect.
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services

</MESSAGE>

Victor

user_input:
To Whom It May Concern,
For context, I have been using the Remote Desktop interactive sessions via the web interface for about a year.  As of this morning, requesting a desktop no longer provides the “Launch Remote Desktop” button.  Please see the attached screenshot. I expected to see the “Launch Remote Desktop” button as shown in the second screenshot. I would appreciate any insights or support. Thank you.
-Pete
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / Remote Desktop Launch Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The missing “Launch Remote Desktop” button can be caused by launch-form settings such as project or account selection, but the screenshots are needed to confirm the exact cause. The safest next step is to review the full launch settings and confirm which project or account is selected.

ACTION
1) Ask the user for a full screenshot of the launch settings if not already available.
2) Confirm that the correct project or account is selected in the Remote Desktop form.
3) If project selection is wrong, explain that the default project may not be appropriate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Pete,

Please send us the full launch settings for the Remote Desktop session, including the selected project/account, so that we can confirm what is preventing the expected button from appearing.

If the current screenshot does not show the full launch form, please send one that includes the relevant project/account fields.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Remote Desktop / Interactive Sessions
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing an issue where the "Launch Remote Desktop" button is not appearing after requesting a desktop session. This issue has been reported by other users as well, where the session jumps straight from "starting" to "completed" without providing the launch option [3][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if the user's account has the necessary permissions and configurations to launch a remote desktop session.
2. Check the OndemandPortal logs for any errors related to the user's session ID.
3. If the issue persists, escalate to the technical team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the session ID for the remote desktop session that did not launch correctly?
- Have you tried launching a remote desktop session from a different browser or device?
- Are there any error messages displayed when the session jumps to "completed"?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Pete,

Thank you for reaching out. We are aware of similar issues where the "Launch Remote Desktop" button does not appear, and the session jumps straight to "completed" [3][4]. Our team will investigate this further.

In the meantime, could you please provide the session ID for the remote desktop session that did not launch correctly? Additionally, have you tried launching a remote desktop session from a different browser or device?

We appreciate your patience and will keep you updated on the progress.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that user permissions and configurations are verified before escalating the issue.
- Document all troubleshooting steps and outcomes for future reference.

REFERENCE KEY
[3] : HPCSSUP-90144::chunk::0001
[4] : HPCSSUP-73321::chunk::0000

user_input:
Hello,
I hope you're well.
I'm a current PhD student and was just made a Data user on the Research Data Store for the Genetics_HPC partition by a collaborator. Upon opening the authentication link, I completely mistakenly clicked "Do not agree", rather than "Agree". The latter was certainly my intention, and now cannot access the drive.
Would there be any way to reverse this and reauthenticate?
Thank you,
Maria

Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user accidentally rejected the storage access invitation by clicking “Do not agree”. Helpdesk cannot reverse that state directly; the storage/data manager who originally added the user must re-add or re-invite them.

ACTION
1) Explain that the original invitation cannot simply be toggled back by helpdesk.
2) Direct the user to the Data Manager or project owner who granted access.
3) Ask that person to re-add/reinvite the user to the storage.

Hello Maria,

In this case the Data Manager who added you to the storage will need to re-add or re-invite you. We cannot reverse that response directly from the helpdesk side.

Best regards,
HPC Support

The user mistakenly clicked "Do not agree" to the terms and conditions for accessing the Research Data Store. This issue has been encountered before and typically requires intervention from the Storage Services Team to reset the agreement status [1][2][3][4].

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN Onboarding / SSH Access / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has been added to a DAWN project but is unsure how to access the service and what credentials to use. The correct onboarding path is to confirm whether the submitted public key has been linked to the DAWN account, complete the separate SSH MFA setup, then connect using the corresponding private key and the DAWN login host.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the public key submitted via the dashboard/email has been linked to the DAWN account.
2) Direct the user to the SSH MFA guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk
3) Provide the DAWN login command:
   `ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
4) Clarify that the SSH client must point to the private key paired with the public key they supplied.
5) Share the DAWN/PVC documentation:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Matteo,

If your public key has already been linked to the DAWN account, the next step is to complete the separate SSH MFA setup and then connect using:
`ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

If your SSH client asks for authentication, make sure it is using the private key that matches the public key you sent us.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: User Onboarding / Access Issues
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has been added to a project and has set up MFA but is unable to find the "Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop" option and is unsure about SSH credentials. The retrieved documents indicate that similar issues have been addressed by providing detailed instructions and confirming SSH key addition [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm that the user's SSH public key has been added to the system.
2. Provide the user with instructions for accessing the "Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop" option via the web interface.
3. Inform the user that SSH access does not require a password but relies on the SSH key and MFA.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Matteo,

Thank you for your message. Your SSH public key has been received and will be added to the system. For SSH access, you do not need a password; instead, you will use your SSH key and MFA.

To access the "Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop" option, please follow these steps:
1. Log in to the web interface at https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/ using your MyAccessID and MFA.
2. Once logged in, navigate to the "Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop" section.
3. Launch a job on the pvc9 partition, requesting at least one GPU on one node, using your project.

For further guidance, please refer to the online documentation: https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#interactive-apps [1][2][3][4][5].

Please let us know if you encounter any further issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user's SSH public key is correctly added to the system before providing further instructions.
- Do not provide passwords for SSH access; SSH access is key-based and requires MFA.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-101079::chunk::0001
[2] : HPCSSUP-95824::chunk::0001
[3] : HPCSSUP-86545::chunk::0001
[4] : HPCSSUP-92808::chunk::0000
[5] : HPCSSUP-98523::chunk::0001

Slurm project: AIRR-P82-DAWN-GPU.
SSH access: You will need to create an SSH key pair and email the public key to [support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk), including the above username. We will append the SSH key to your user account and send further instructions.
Web interface access: Please let us know if you wish to use the web interface ( [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk)](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk)) and we will send you a link to set up your password.
